answer.stepNumbering: 100-1600 in hundreds for chromatic ramps; gray and the transparent ramps insert 25/50/75 and stop at 1000

summary: The S2 base palette (color-palette.json, 372 tokens) is 21 full stepped ramps: 18 chromatic families (blue, red, orange, yellow, chartreuse, celery, green, seafoam, cyan, indigo, purple, fuchsia, magenta, pink, turquoise, brown, silver, cinnamon) with 16 steps numbered 100-1600, plus gray, transparent-white and transparent-black with 13 steps (25, 50, 75, 100-1000). 11 partial static-* ramps (mode-invariant colors), black, white and three avatar gradient stops round out the file. Every palette token is marked private: true. No per-step role table is published; guidance stays at group level ('prefer semantic colors over global colors').

evidence.excerpt: "blue-800": {"$schema": "https://opensource.adobe.com/spectrum-design-data/schemas/token-types/color-set.json", "uuid": "85be576c-6810-4971-9748-e558384b903d", "private": true, "sets": {"light": {"value": "rgb(75, 117, 255)"} ...
evidence.note: Counted from the file: 372 tokens; 18 chromatic families at 16 steps (100-1600); gray, transparent-white, transparent-black at 13 steps (25-1000); 11 static-* partial ramps; black/white and gradient-stop-{1,2,3}-avatar singletons. All 372 tokens carry private: true.

evidence.excerpt: Global colors: The following values are available across all color properties. gray-25 gray-50 gray-75 gray-100 [...] gray-1000 blue-100 [...] blue-1600 [...] Transparent colors: These colors are useful for overlays or transparent effects. transparent transparent-white-25 [...] transparent-black-1000 transparent-overlay-25 [...]
evidence.note: First-party S2 reference enumerating the palette exposed to product code; transparent-overlay-* is a semantic overlay ramp on top of the two palette alpha ramps.

evidence.excerpt: Prefer to use semantic colors over global colors when they represent a specific meaning. [...] These colors are specifically chosen to be used as backgrounds, so prefer them over global colors where possible.
evidence.note: The only published usage guidance is at this group level; nothing assigns roles to individual steps (unlike e.g. Radix's per-step table).

notes: rampCount 21 counts full ramps only; stepsPerRamp 16 is the dominant (chromatic) count, with gray and the two transparent ramps at 13.

answer.algorithm: Leonardo: contrast-ratio-targeted generation - key colors are interpolated into a full lightness scale in a selectable color space, then swatch values are solved to hit target WCAG-style contrast ratios against a background color
inputs: colorspace (interpolation space: LCH, LAB, CAM02, HSL, HSLuv, HSV, RGB)

summary: Adobe's generation tool Leonardo (@adobe/leonardo-contrast-colors, Apache-2.0) produces scales by interpolating key colors and solving each swatch to a target contrast ratio; Adobe documented this 'adaptive color palette' approach for Spectrum 1's color rewrite. No S2-labeled source states that the S2 palette was produced this way - the tokens repo ships only literal rgb() values with no generation code and no Leonardo reference - so the S2 connection is continuity-inferred, corroborated by measured S2 values landing at WCAG thresholds (see contrast.strategy).

evidence.excerpt: Authoring adaptive color palettes for generating color based on a desired contrast ratio. [...] to make it easier for designers and engineers to conform to WCAG minimum contrast standards by using contrast ratio as the starting point, rather than a post-color-selection auditing process

| `colorKeys` | Array of strings | List of specific colors to interpolate between in order to generate a full lightness scale of the color. | | `colorspace` | Enum | The colorspace in which the key colors will be interpolated within. | | `ratios` | Array or Object | List of target contrast ratios, or object with named keys for each value.
evidence.note: Theme/Color/BackgroundColor API; colorspace options are LCH, LAB, CAM02, HSL, HSLuv, HSV, RGB.

evidence.excerpt: we rewrote documentation and engineered colors as an adaptive color palette (a systematic approach in which we define the constraints informing color generation rather than create static swatches)
evidence.note: About Spectrum 1's color rewrite, which predates S2 - cited as lineage, not as an S2 statement.
The S2 token source carries only literal rgb()/rgba() values; the repo contains no generation code and its READMEs never mention Leonardo or color generation.

notes: Inferred from S1 lineage: Leonardo remains Adobe's open generation tool and the S1 adaptive-color methodology is first-party documented, but S2 publishes no generation story of its own. spectrum.adobe.com still serves S1 docs (its color pages are pinned to colors v6.0.0) and s2.spectrum.adobe.com is a marketing microsite with no color documentation - deliberately not cited as S2 evidence.

summary: S2 ships exclusively 8-bit sRGB: the full @adobe/spectrum-tokens dist (2,469 tokens) contains only rgb()/rgba() literals - zero display-p3, oklch, lab or lch occurrences - and the @react-spectrum/s2 style macro compiles them to hex in its atomic CSS output. The macro's colorMix() utility mixes 'in sRGB color space'. The authoring space behind the S2 values is not published; Leonardo (the S1-documented tool) interpolates in selectable spaces, but no S2 source states which was used.

evidence.excerpt: "focus-indicator-color": {"ref": "{blue-800}", "sets": {"light": {"value": "rgb(75, 117, 255)" [...]}, "dark": {"value": "rgb(64, 105, 253)" [...]}, "wireframe": {"value": "rgb(93, 127, 201)" [...]}}}
evidence.note: Shipped npm dist consumed by @react-spectrum/s2 (per-value uuids elided). Grep across all 2,469 tokens: 3,000 rgb( and 163 rgba( values; no display-p3/oklch/lab()/lch() anywhere.

evidence.excerpt: colorMix - Mixes two Spectrum colors by a given percentage using CSS color-mix() in sRGB color space.
The style macro runs at build time and returns a class name that applies Spectrum 2 design tokens [...] .bJ { background-color : #ffbcb4 } . ac { color : #fff }
evidence.note: Atomic CSS output resolves token values to hex.

notes: workingSpaces is null (not published for S2). S1 documented CAM02-UCS-based work and Leonardo supports multiple interpolation spaces, but copying that claim to S2 would be inference; the shipped-format and wide-gamut findings are directly verified.

summary: No S2-labeled source publishes contrast guarantees, targets, or a per-step table - guaranteedPairings is empty because nothing is promised for S2 in writing. By-construction WCAG 2.x enforcement is inferred from lineage (Leonardo generates colors to hit WCAG-style ratios; Adobe documented that approach for Spectrum 1 v6) and corroborated by measurement: computed from the shipped S2 token values, neutral-content-color-default on background-base-color is 14.55:1 (light) / 13.64:1 (dark), and accent-background-color-default against white is 4.81:1 (light) / 4.51:1 (dark) - the dark value landing a hair above WCAG AA's 4.5:1 threshold is characteristic of ratio-targeted generation, not hand-picking.

evidence.excerpt: to make it easier for designers and engineers to conform to WCAG minimum contrast standards by using contrast ratio as the starting point, rather than a post-color-selection auditing process
evidence.note: Tool-level enforcement philosophy; not an S2-labeled statement.

evidence.excerpt: "accent-background-color-default": {"sets": {"light": {"value": "{accent-color-900}"}, "dark": {"value": "{accent-color-800}"}, "wireframe": {"value": "{accent-color-900}"}}} [...] "neutral-content-color-default": {"value": "{gray-800}"} [...] "background-base-color": {"value": "{gray-25}"}
evidence.note: Observed, not official: WCAG 2.x relative-luminance ratios computed from these resolved values on the retrieval date - neutral text on base background 14.55:1 light / 13.64:1 dark; accent background vs white 4.81:1 light / 4.51:1 dark.

evidence.excerpt: we define the constraints informing color generation rather than create static swatches
evidence.note: S1 lineage for the by-construction stance.

notes: Enforcement and standard are inferred (S1 lineage + measured threshold behavior), never stated for S2. No automated contrast checking was found in the spectrum-design-data repo. Forced-colors/high-contrast is handled separately by delegating to OS system colors (see modes.support).

summary: Three layers, cleanly separated by file in the token source: color-palette.json holds raw per-mode rgb() values and marks every token private: true; semantic-color-palette.json remaps whole scales onto concepts (accent-color-100..1600 = blue steps) while color-aliases.json names purposes with state suffixes (accent-background-color-default, neutral-content-color-default, background-base-color); color-component.json holds ~73 component-scoped tokens carrying an explicit component field. The full palette being private is the architectural statement: product code is steered to the semantic and component layers.

evidence.excerpt: "blue-800": {"$schema": "https://opensource.adobe.com/spectrum-design-data/schemas/token-types/color-set.json", "uuid": "85be576c-6810-4971-9748-e558384b903d", "private": true, "sets": {"light": {"value": "rgb(75, 117, 255)"} ...
"accent-color-800": {"$schema": "https://opensource.adobe.com/spectrum-design-data/schemas/token-types/alias.json", "value": "{blue-800}", "uuid": "87a2c8f0-54fd-4939-8f42-3124fde1e49e"}

"avatar-border-color": {"$schema": "https://opensource.adobe.com/spectrum-design-data/schemas/token-types/alias.json", "component": "avatar", "value": "{gray-25}", "uuid": "bae79ea7-2b3e-4d43-af42-7deab67acc7c"}

notes: Layer names are the token source's own file/set names; Adobe publishes no S2 glossary labeling them (the primitive/semantic/component kinds are this schema's classification, read off the structure: literal values vs {alias} references vs an explicit component field).

summary: The S2 token source is exactly 8 files (manifest.json): four color files plus typography.json (312 tokens), layout.json (359 tokens: spacing/gap, corner-radius-*, drop-shadow-* for elevation), layout-component.json (1,010 per-component dimension tokens) and icons.json (79). Opacity is a first-class token type used by component tokens (avatar-opacity-disabled, table-row-hover-opacity) though it has no dedicated file. No motion, breakpoint, cursor or z-index tokens exist anywhere in the source.

evidence.excerpt: ["src/typography.json","src/semantic-color-palette.json","src/layout.json","src/layout-component.json","src/icons.json","src/color-palette.json","src/color-component.json","src/color-aliases.json"]
evidence.note: Authoritative full file list; the absence claims (motion, breakpoints, cursor, z-index) rest on grepping all 8 files' token names with zero matches for duration/animation/easing/breakpoint/cursor/z-index.

evidence.excerpt: corner-radius-0, corner-radius-100, corner-radius-1000 [...] drop-shadow-blur, drop-shadow-blur-100 [...] base-gap-large
evidence.note: Token names from the file's keys: radius, elevation (drop-shadow) and spacing co-located in layout.json.

notes: Breakpoints exist in the @react-spectrum/s2 style macro as conditions (sm/md/lg/xl/2xl media queries) but are implementation constants, not design tokens. Elevation is tokenized as drop-shadow-* parts rather than a named elevation scale.

answer.pattern: semantic aliases read {concept}-{role}-color-{state} (accent-background-color-default, neutral-content-color-hover); palette reads {family}-{step} (blue-800); component tokens read {component}-{property} (avatar-border-color)

summary: All token names are kebab-case with a consistent concept-role-property-state grammar at the alias layer. Delivery is a build-time style macro: @react-spectrum/s2's Parcel macro reads @adobe/spectrum-tokens' variables.json at build time and emits atomic CSS classes. Color modes are switched with the native CSS light-dark() function driven by the color-scheme property (no class or data-attribute toggling); forced-colors/high-contrast styles apply under @media (forced-colors: active). A small set of --s2-* custom properties (scale, fonts) rides along in the emitted CSS.

evidence.excerpt: "accent-background-color-default": {"sets": {"light": {"value": "{accent-color-900}"}, "dark": {"value": "{accent-color-800}"} ...}} / "neutral-background-color-default": {"value": "{gray-800}"}
token.light === token.dark ? token.light : `light-dark(${token.light}, ${token.dark})`; ... colorScheme: ['light', 'dark', 'light dark'] as const,
evidence.note: Native CSS light-dark() is the light/dark switching mechanism.

evidence.excerpt: import * as originalTokens from '@adobe/spectrum-tokens/dist/json/variables.json'; ... export function getToken(name: TokenName): string {
evidence.note: Build-time consumption of the token package inside the style macro; the macro must be imported `with {type: 'macro'}`.

evidence.excerpt: Conditions: forcedColors Values '@media (forced-colors: active)' [...] High contrast mode colors - These colors map to system colors when forced colors mode is active. Background ButtonBorder ButtonFace ButtonText Field Highlight HighlightText GrayText Mark LinkText

notes: css-light-dark was added to the question bank's themingMechanisms enum for this system (additive): S2 is the first researched system whose mode switching is the native CSS light-dark() function rather than custom properties re-pointed by class/attribute.

summary: Every mode-aware token carries three literal value sets - light, dark, wireframe - on the same token (301 of 372 palette tokens; static-* and transparent-* are mode-invariant by design). Dark is its own resolved palette, not a runtime transform. @react-spectrum/s2 renders light/dark via CSS light-dark() and the Provider's colorScheme prop 'defaults to operating system preferences'. High contrast is a dedicated forced-colors mapping: color values swap to OS system color keywords (Highlight, ButtonFace, GrayText...) under @media (forced-colors: active). The wireframe set ships at token level but the style macro's lightDark() utility only exposes light and dark.

evidence.note: 301 of 372 palette tokens carry the light/dark/wireframe set triple; the other 71 (static-*, transparent-*, black, white, avatar gradient stops) are single-value.

evidence.excerpt: colorScheme ColorScheme - The color scheme for your application. Defaults to operating system preferences.

High contrast mode colors - These colors map to system colors when forced colors mode is active. Background ButtonBorder ButtonFace ButtonText Field Highlight HighlightText GrayText Mark LinkText [...] lightDark - Produces a light-dark() CSS color value that resolves to different colors depending on the current color scheme.

notes: highContrast is 'dedicated-mode' in the sense of a fully-handled forced-colors mode that delegates color values to the OS - S2 ships the mapping, not a high-contrast palette of its own. S1's darkest theme is gone; wireframe replaced it as the third token set.

answer.colorSource: 'focus-ring' style value = focus-indicator-color token = {blue-800} (light rgb(75, 117, 255) / dark rgb(64, 105, 253)); Highlight system color under forced-colors

summary: S2 centralizes the focus ring in a shared focusRing() style-macro helper that interactive components spread into their styles: a CSS outline that is 'none' by default and 'solid' only under React Aria's isFocusVisible render prop, with outlineWidth 2 and outlineOffset 2. The color is the 'focus-ring' theme value, mapped to the focus-indicator-color token, which references palette step blue-800; the token dist also carries focus-indicator-thickness: 2px and focus-indicator-gap: 2px matching the helper's values. Under forced-colors the ring color becomes the OS Highlight keyword.

evidence.excerpt: export const focusRing = () => ({ outlineStyle: { default: 'none', isFocusVisible: 'solid' }, outlineColor: { default: 'focus-ring', forcedColors: 'Highlight' }, outlineWidth: 2, outlineOffset: 2 }) as const;
evidence.note: JSDoc: 'Returns consistent Spectrum focus ring outline styles for interactive components. Note: Requires `isFocusVisible` to be passed into the style call that uses this.'

evidence.excerpt: outlineColor: new SpectrumColorProperty('outlineColor', { ...baseColors, 'focus-ring': { ...colorToken('focus-indicator-color'), forcedColors: 'Highlight' } }),

"focus-indicator-color": {"ref": "{blue-800}", "sets": {"light": {"value": "rgb(75, 117, 255)" [...]}, "dark": {"value": "rgb(64, 105, 253)" [...]}, "wireframe": {"value": "rgb(93, 127, 201)" [...]}}}
evidence.note: Per-value uuids elided. Same dist defines focus-indicator-thickness: 2px and focus-indicator-gap: 2px.

notes: Button.tsx overrides the ring color to a transparent-overlay token when staticColor is set (rings over photos/colored surfaces).

summary: Button.tsx defines 6 fixed variants crossed with fillStyle ('fill' | 'outline') and staticColor ('white' | 'black' | 'auto'). Its styles mix semantic values (baseColor('neutral'), 'disabled', 'focus-ring', accent-900/negative-900 alias-scale steps) with raw primitive steps (gray-100, gray-300, gray-800, and fuchsia/indigo/blue-900 stops in the premium/genai gradients) - the semantic layer is preferred but not enforced. There is no hue axis: accentOptions is null because the accent hue is fixed by the token theme (accent = blue), not a Button prop. Zero raw color literals appear in the file; only token names, 'transparent', and forced-colors system keywords.

evidence.excerpt: variant?: 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'accent' | 'negative' | 'premium' | 'genai'; /** The background style of the Button. @default 'fill' */ fillStyle?: 'fill' | 'outline';
evidence.note: ButtonStyleProps; staticColor?: 'white' | 'black' | 'auto' - 'The static color style to apply. Useful when the Button appears over a color background.'

evidence.excerpt: backgroundColor: { fillStyle: { fill: { variant: { primary: baseColor('neutral'), secondary: baseColor('gray-100'), accent: { default: lightDark('accent-900', 'accent-700'), isHovered: lightDark('accent-1000', 'accent-600'), ...
evidence.note: Semantic (neutral) and primitive (gray-100) values side by side; premium/genai use linearGradient over fuchsia/indigo/blue/red/magenta palette steps - tokens, never literals.

evidence.excerpt: const button = style<ButtonRenderProps & ButtonStyleProps & {isStaticColor: boolean}>( { ...focusRing(), ...staticColor(), ...control({shape: 'pill', wrap: true, icon: true}),
evidence.note: Button composes the shared focusRing()/staticColor() helpers.

notes: No component-layer color tokens (color-component.json defines none for Button); the style macro resolves names like 'neutral' to semantic tokens (neutral-background-color-default) and gray-100/accent-900 to palette/alias steps at build time.
